doc.title: Year-end bond interest accrual
doc.event: The bonds pay each October 1 and April 1, but the books close December 31 — three months into a six-month coupon period. The adjusting entry accrues exactly the elapsed fraction of expense, payable, and amortization.

entries.caption: Dec 31 adjusting entry (issued Apr 1, coupons Oct 1 / Apr 1, at a discount)

lines.label: 3/6 of the period's expense
lines.why: Matching: three of the six months in the Oct 1 → Apr 1 period belong to THIS fiscal year, so this year takes three months of the period's expense — the cash slice plus the amortization slice.
lines.trap: Accruing the full six months (or none) is the classic year-end error — the fraction is the whole point.

lines.label: 3/6 of the period's amortization
lines.why: Amortization doesn't wait for the coupon date — the carrying value walks toward face continuously, so the accrual takes its three-month share.
lines.trap: Skipping amortization in the accrual understates expense and leaves the carrying value stale at year-end.

lines.label: 3/6 of the cash coupon
lines.why: Three months of the contractual coupon is owed but unpaid at Dec 31 — a liability, not cash.
lines.trap: No Cash line in an accrual. Cash moves on the coupon date, not at year-end.

computationPaths.narration: Take period 2's full-period numbers from the schedule and multiply each by 3/6 (Oct 1 → Dec 31). Expense is built as payable-portion + amortization-portion, so the entry balances by construction.

variants.label: Premium bonds — same fraction, amortization flips sides

lines.label: payable − premium slice
lines.why: At a premium the amortization REDUCES expense, so the accrued expense is the payable slice minus the premium slice.
lines.trap: Adding the premium slice instead of subtracting flips you into the discount pattern.

lines.label: 3/6 of the amortization
lines.why: Three months of premium burns off — debited, walking the carrying value down toward face.
lines.trap: Premium amortizes with a DEBIT; crediting it grows the liability.

lines.label: 3/6 of the cash coupon
lines.why: Same contractual accrual as always: three months of coupon owed.
lines.trap: The payable is a function of the CONTRACT (face × stated) — the premium never changes it.

memorize.body: Accrual = each of the period's numbers × elapsed months / period months. Payable from the cash column, amortization from the amortization column, expense = their sum (discount) or difference (premium).

Then don't double it: the NEXT coupon entry must recognize only the REMAINING months of expense and clear the payable — or reverse the accrual first. Exams test the follow-through, not just the accrual.

questions.prompt: Discount bonds pay Oct 1 / Apr 1; the year ends Dec 31. What is interest expense in the Dec 31 adjusting entry?

distractors.feedback: That's only the payable slice. At a discount, three months of amortization also belongs in this year's expense.

traces.label: Period-2 schedule row ↔ the 3/6 slices in the entry

build.scaffold: Ask two questions: how many months of the current coupon period fall before year-end (that's your fraction), and which direction does the amortization push expense (discount adds, premium subtracts). No cash moves at year-end.
